words.shortDefinition: The protocol browsers and servers use to exchange data on the web.
words.simpleDefinition: The foundational protocol browsers and servers use to exchange data — defining how messages are formatted and transmitted across the internet. It acts as a standardized language for requesting resources and receiving responses in a stateless manner.
words.longDefinition: HyperText Transfer Protocol defines how clients request resources and servers respond. It is stateless and text-based. HTTP/1.1 introduced persistent connections; HTTP/2 added multiplexing; HTTP/3 runs over QUIC for lower latency.
words.connector: Think of HTTP like ordering at a restaurant — you make a specific request and the kitchen sends back a matching response.
words.techContext: Every web API call is HTTP. Understanding methods, status codes, headers, and keep-alive is essential for debugging network issues.
words.exampleSentence: The API was returning HTTP 200 even on errors, making client-side error handling nearly impossible.
words.etymology: coined by Tim Berners-Lee at CERN in 1989 alongside HTML and the first web server.

codeExample.code:
GET /users/42 HTTP/1.1
Host: api.example.com
Authorization: Bearer <token>

→ HTTP/1.1 200 OK
→ Content-Type: application/json

words.shortDefinition: An architectural style for APIs that uses HTTP methods and resource-based URLs.
words.simpleDefinition: Architectural style for web APIs where every piece of data has a unique address — enabling interaction through standard HTTP verbs like GET, POST, and DELETE. Emphasizes statelessness and a uniform interface for scalable web services.
words.longDefinition: Representational State Transfer defines six constraints — statelessness, uniform interface, client-server separation, cacheability, layered system, and optional code on demand. A RESTful API maps CRUD operations to HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) on noun-based endpoints.
words.connector: Think of REST like a filing cabinet — each folder has a fixed location and you use standard actions to manage the contents.
words.techContext: REST is the dominant API style for web services. Its stateless nature makes it horizontally scalable by default.
words.exampleSentence: We designed the API following REST conventions so each resource had a predictable URL and the correct HTTP method.
words.etymology: coined by Roy Fielding in his 2000 PhD dissertation at UC Irvine.

codeExample.code:
GET    /articles        # list
POST   /articles        # create
GET    /articles/42     # read
PUT    /articles/42     # update
DELETE /articles/42     # delete

words.shortDefinition: A defined contract that lets one program communicate with another.
words.simpleDefinition: Formal contract allowing two different software programs to communicate — specifying exactly how to request information and what results to expect. It enables services to interact without needing to know each other's internal implementation details.
words.longDefinition: An Application Programming Interface specifies how software components interact: what operations are available, what inputs they accept, and what outputs they return. Web APIs use HTTP; library APIs expose functions; OS APIs expose system calls.
words.connector: Think of an API like a TV remote — you press a button and the TV responds, without you needing to know the circuitry.
words.techContext: APIs are everywhere — you consume them (third-party services) and produce them (your backend). Good API design is about clarity and stability.
words.exampleSentence: We versioned the API so breaking changes in v2 didn't affect clients still using v1.
words.etymology: first appeared in IBM documentation in the 1960s; popularised in web development in the 2000s.

words.shortDefinition: A specific URL at which an API is accessible and responds to requests.
words.simpleDefinition: Specific URL and HTTP method combination where an API receives requests for a particular resource — serving as the digital point of entry. Each endpoint represents a discrete piece of business logic or data access.
words.longDefinition: An endpoint is the combination of an HTTP method and a URL path that maps to a handler function. Well-designed endpoints use plural nouns (/users), avoid verbs (/getUser), and nest resources logically (/users/42/orders).
words.connector: Think of an endpoint like a specific counter at the DMV — each counter handles one task, and you must go to the right one.
words.techContext: Endpoint design decisions (naming, versioning, nesting depth) are hard to change once clients depend on them.
words.exampleSentence: The mobile team was hitting the wrong endpoint because the staging URL had not been updated in the config.
words.etymology: compound of end + point; adopted from networking (cable termination) to mean an API access point.

words.shortDefinition: A browser security mechanism that controls which origins can make requests to a different domain.
words.simpleDefinition: Browser security mechanism controlling which external websites can request resources from your server — preventing unauthorized cross-origin data access. It uses HTTP headers to declare which domains are permitted to interact with the backend services.
words.longDefinition: Cross-Origin Resource Sharing lets servers declare which external domains may access their resources via HTTP headers. Browsers enforce the same-origin policy by default; CORS headers opt out of that restriction. Preflight OPTIONS requests are sent for non-simple requests.
words.connector: Think of CORS like a bouncer at a club — your browser asks 'is this request on the guest list?' before letting it pass.
words.techContext: CORS errors are one of the most common frustrations in web development. The fix is always server-side — add the right Access-Control-Allow-Origin header.
words.exampleSentence: The frontend was blocked by a CORS error because the backend hadn't whitelisted the staging domain.
words.etymology: acronym coined by W3C in the 2009 CORS specification draft.

words.shortDefinition: A small piece of data a server sends to the browser to persist state across requests.
words.simpleDefinition: Small piece of data stored in the browser by a server to maintain state across different requests — typically used for tracking sessions or user preferences. Cookies are automatically sent with subsequent matching requests.
words.longDefinition: Cookies are key-value pairs stored in the browser and automatically sent with every matching HTTP request. They carry session IDs, preferences, and tracking identifiers. Important attributes: HttpOnly (no JS access), Secure (HTTPS only), SameSite (CSRF protection), and Max-Age (expiry).
words.connector: Think of a cookie like a cloakroom ticket — you get one when you check in and hand it back to prove it's still you.
words.techContext: Session cookies expire when the browser closes; persistent cookies last until Max-Age. HttpOnly and Secure flags are security essentials.
words.exampleSentence: We set the session cookie with HttpOnly and Secure flags to prevent XSS from stealing it.
words.etymology: named by Lou Montulli at Netscape (1994) after the computing term 'magic cookie' — a token passed between programs.

words.shortDefinition: An HTTP callback that lets one service notify another when an event occurs.
words.simpleDefinition: Event-driven notification system where one service pushes data to another automatically upon a specific occurrence — eliminating the need for constant polling. It uses HTTP POST requests to deliver real-time updates directly to a URL.
words.longDefinition: Webhooks invert the polling model — instead of your app asking 'did anything happen?', the source service pushes an HTTP POST to your URL when an event fires. They power Stripe payment confirmations, GitHub CI triggers, and Slack notifications.
words.connector: Think of a webhook like a doorbell — instead of checking the door every minute, the system pings you the moment someone arrives.
words.techContext: Always verify webhook signatures to prevent spoofed events. Return 200 quickly and process asynchronously to avoid delivery timeouts.
words.exampleSentence: We processed Stripe payments via webhook so our database updated the moment the charge succeeded, not when the user navigated back.
words.etymology: coined by Jeff Lindsay in 2007 in a blog post proposing 'web hooks' as a reverse HTTP pattern.

words.shortDefinition: A function that sits in the request-response pipeline and can inspect, modify, or terminate requests.
words.simpleDefinition: Reusable logic sitting within the request-response cycle — handling cross-cutting concerns like authentication, logging, or body parsing before requests reach the final route handler. Enables modular architecture by separating infrastructure tasks from business logic.
words.longDefinition: Middleware chains functions that each receive the request, optionally transform it, then pass it to the next handler. Common uses: authentication, logging, rate limiting, compression, and CORS. The order of middleware matters — auth must run before protected routes.
words.connector: Think of middleware like a conveyor belt at airport security — every bag passes through the same checks before reaching the gate.
words.techContext: Thinking of a web server as a middleware pipeline helps keep cross-cutting concerns (auth, logging) separate from business logic.
words.exampleSentence: We added a middleware that logged every request's duration so we could spot slow endpoints in production.
words.etymology: from mid- + software; originally described software gluing mainframes to applications (1960s), repurposed for web frameworks in the 2000s.

words.shortDefinition: A lightweight text format for representing structured data as key-value pairs and arrays.
words.simpleDefinition: Lightweight, language-agnostic data format representing structured information as readable text key-value pairs — serving as the primary standard for modern API communication. Its simplicity and native compatibility with JavaScript make it ideal for web development.
words.longDefinition: JavaScript Object Notation is language-agnostic despite its name. It supports strings, numbers, booleans, null, objects, and arrays. JSON replaced XML as the dominant web data format due to its readability and native JavaScript compatibility.
words.connector: Think of JSON like a standardised form — everyone fills in the same labelled boxes so any system can read it clearly.
words.techContext: Used for REST API payloads, config files, and data storage. JSON has no support for comments, dates, or binary — common gotchas.
words.exampleSentence: The API returned a JSON payload with a nested user object, but the mobile client expected a flat structure.
words.etymology: introduced and popularised by Douglas Crockford around 2001, standardised as ECMA-404 in 2013.

codeExample.code:
{
  "id": 42,
  "name": "Ada Lovelace",
  "roles": ["admin", "user"],
  "active": true,
  "metadata": null
}

words.shortDefinition: A three-digit number in an HTTP response indicating whether the request succeeded or failed.
words.simpleDefinition: Three-digit numerical result returned by a server indicating whether an HTTP request succeeded or why it failed — categorized into classes like 2xx for success and 4xx for client errors. Critical for handling API outcomes.
words.longDefinition: HTTP status codes are grouped by first digit: 2xx (success), 3xx (redirect), 4xx (client error), 5xx (server error). Returning the correct code is critical — returning 200 on errors causes clients to mishandle failures.
words.connector: Think of status codes like a traffic light — green means go, yellow means follow a redirect, and red means stop for an error.
words.techContext: Common codes every developer must know: 200 OK, 201 Created, 400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized, 403 Forbidden, 404 Not Found, 422 Unprocessable Entity, 429 Too Many Requests, 500 Internal Server Error.
words.exampleSentence: The client was retrying on 400 errors, which it should never do — 400 means the request itself is wrong.
words.etymology: standardised by IETF in RFC 2616 (1999), evolved through subsequent RFCs.

words.shortDefinition: Uniform Resource Locator - the address of a resource on the web.
words.simpleDefinition: Uniform Resource Locator — the unique global address used to identify and locate a specific resource on the internet. It specifies the protocol, domain name, and path needed to retrieve data or load a web page.
words.longDefinition: A URL specifies the protocol, domain, port, path, query string, and fragment needed to locate a resource. Format: protocol://domain:port/path?query#fragment. URLs are a subset of URIs (Uniform Resource Identifiers).
words.connector: Think of a URL like a postal address — the protocol is the delivery method, the domain the building, and the path the apartment.
words.techContext: URL encoding transforms special characters into %XX format. Understanding URL structure is essential for routing, security, and debugging.
words.exampleSentence: The redirect failed because we forgot to URL-encode the query parameter, breaking the URL when it contained an ampersand.
words.etymology: coined by Tim Berners-Lee in 1994; formalized in RFC 1738.

codeExample.code:
https://api.example.com:443/users/42?include=orders&format=json#profile

protocol: https
domain:   api.example.com
port:     443
path:     /users/42
query:    include=orders&format=json
fragment: profile

words.shortDefinition: A key-value pair appended to a URL after a question mark to pass data to the server.
words.simpleDefinition: Optional key-value pair appended to the end of a URL after a question mark — allowing clients to pass extra information, like search filters or sorting rules, to the server. Facilitates customizable and bookmarkable requests.
words.longDefinition: Query parameters provide optional inputs to an endpoint without changing the path. Multiple parameters are separated by &. They're visible in URLs, logged by proxies, and should never contain sensitive data. Used for filtering, pagination, and search.
words.connector: Think of query parameters like pizza toppings — the base pizza stays the same, but you append extras to customise the final order.
words.techContext: Query params are idempotent (safe for GET requests). For non-idempotent operations or sensitive data, use the request body.
words.exampleSentence: We paginated the results using query parameters like ?page=2&limit=20 so clients could bookmark specific pages.
words.etymology: query from Latin quaerere ("to seek"); parameter from Greek para- ("beside") + metron ("measure").

codeExample.code:
GET /api/users?role=admin&active=true&page=2 HTTP/1.1

# Query params:
# role=admin
# active=true
# page=2

words.shortDefinition: Metadata sent with an HTTP request or response to provide context about the message.
words.simpleDefinition: Metadata accompanying HTTP requests and responses providing essential context about the message body, sender, and desired handling — such as authentication tokens or content type. It serves as the instructional preamble before the actual data payload.
words.longDefinition: Headers carry information like content type, authentication tokens, caching directives, and accepted encodings. Request headers (e.g., Authorization, User-Agent) come from the client; response headers (e.g., Content-Type, Set-Cookie) come from the server.
words.connector: Think of HTTP headers like the cover sheet on a fax — it describes the contents and sender before you reach the actual document.
words.techContext: Essential headers: Content-Type (payload format), Authorization (credentials), Cache-Control (caching rules), Accept (preferred format).
words.exampleSentence: The API rejected the request because we sent a JSON body but forgot the Content-Type: application/json header.
words.etymology: from head + -er; in HTTP, headers come at the 'head' of the message before the body.

codeExample.code:
POST /api/users HTTP/1.1
Host: api.example.com
Content-Type: application/json
Authorization: Bearer eyJhbGc...

{"name": "Ada"}

words.shortDefinition: Verifying the identity of a user or system making a request.
words.simpleDefinition: The formal process of verifying the identity of a user or system making a request — typically through credentials like passwords, tokens, or keys. It answers the fundamental security question: 'Who are you?'.
words.longDefinition: Authentication answers 'who are you?' Common mechanisms: username/password, API keys, JWTs, OAuth tokens, biometrics. Authentication precedes authorization — you must prove your identity before the system checks your permissions.
words.connector: Think of authentication like unlocking your phone — you prove it is you before the system lets you access your private data.
words.techContext: Always use HTTPS for authentication to prevent credential interception. Multi-factor authentication adds a second proof beyond passwords.
words.exampleSentence: The login flow uses password authentication first, then sends a JWT for subsequent API requests.
words.etymology: from Greek authentikos ("genuine") from authentes ("one acting on one's own authority").

codeExample.code:
# Session-based auth:
POST /login HTTP/1.1
{"email": "ada@example.com", "password": "..."}

→ Set-Cookie: session=abc123; HttpOnly; Secure

words.shortDefinition: Determining what actions an authenticated user is permitted to perform.
words.simpleDefinition: Process of determining whether an authenticated user has permission to perform a specific action or access a resource. It answers the fundamental security question: 'What are you allowed to do?' based on roles or rules.
words.longDefinition: Authorization answers 'what can you do?' It happens after authentication. Common models: role-based (admin, user), permission-based (can_edit, can_delete), or attribute-based. Returns HTTP 403 Forbidden if denied.
words.connector: Think of authorization like a permission slip — it's not about who you are, it's about which specific rooms you're allowed to enter.
words.techContext: Confused deputy problem occurs when authorization checks are missing or inconsistent across endpoints.
words.exampleSentence: Even though the user authenticated successfully, authorization failed because they lacked the admin role required to delete accounts.
words.etymology: from Latin auctor ("originator, promoter") + -ize + -ation — granting authority.

words.shortDefinition: A period of interaction between a user and a server, tracked via a session identifier.
words.simpleDefinition: Temporary period of interaction between a user and server — enabling the system to remember user context across multiple requests despite HTTP's stateless nature. It usually relies on a unique session ID stored in a cookie.
words.longDefinition: Sessions persist user state across multiple HTTP requests. The server stores session data (user ID, cart items) and sends a session ID to the client as a cookie. The client includes this ID with subsequent requests. Sessions expire after timeout or logout.
words.connector: Think of a session like a tab at a bar — the bartender remembers you for the evening without you re-introducing yourself for every drink.
words.techContext: Session storage can be in-memory, database, or Redis. Horizontal scaling requires sticky sessions or shared session storage.
words.exampleSentence: The user's cart was empty after load balancing sent them to a different server because we weren't using shared session storage.
words.etymology: from Latin sessio ("a sitting") from sedere ("to sit") — the time spent 'sitting' with the application.

words.shortDefinition: JSON Web Token - a self-contained token that carries user claims and is cryptographically signed.
words.simpleDefinition: JSON Web Token — a self-contained, digitally signed token used to transmit identity and authorization claims securely between parties. Its stateless nature allows servers to verify user permissions without needing to query a central database for each request.
words.longDefinition: JWTs encode user identity and permissions in a JSON payload, then sign it with a secret or private key. The client stores the JWT and sends it with each request. The server verifies the signature without querying a database. JWTs are stateless but can't be revoked without additional infrastructure.
words.connector: Think of a JWT like a signed concert wristband — anyone can verify it is genuine and it shows your access level immediately.
words.techContext: Common for API authentication and SSO. Three parts: header.payload.signature. Never store sensitive data in the payload — it's base64, not encrypted.
words.exampleSentence: We switched from sessions to JWTs so our stateless API could scale horizontally without session affinity.
words.etymology: defined in RFC 7519 (2015); widely adopted for stateless authentication.

codeExample.code:
# JWT structure:
eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9.      # header
eyJzdWIiOiIxMjM0NTY3ODkwIiwibmFtZSI6IkFkYSJ9.  # payload
SflKxwRJSMeKKF2QT4fwpMeJf36POk6yJV_adQssw5c      # signature

# Client sends:
Authorization: Bearer <jwt>

words.shortDefinition: HTTP Secure - HTTP encrypted with TLS to protect data in transit.
words.simpleDefinition: The secure, encrypted version of HTTP using TLS to protect data transmitted between a browser and a server. It ensures privacy and data integrity, protecting sensitive information like login credentials from eavesdropping and man-in-the-middle attacks.
words.longDefinition: HTTPS wraps HTTP traffic in TLS encryption, preventing eavesdropping and tampering. Requires an SSL/TLS certificate. Browsers show a padlock icon and warn users when connections aren't secure. Essential for transmitting passwords, tokens, or personal data.
words.connector: Think of HTTPS vs HTTP like a sealed envelope vs a postcard — HTTPS seals the content so only the recipient can read it.
words.techContext: Always use HTTPS in production. Modern browsers block features like geolocation and camera access on HTTP sites.
words.exampleSentence: We enforce HTTPS by redirecting all HTTP requests to port 443 and setting the HSTS header to prevent downgrade attacks.
words.etymology: HTTPS adopted in 1994 by Netscape; TLS (Transport Layer Security) replaced SSL in 1999.

words.shortDefinition: A protocol for persistent, bidirectional communication between client and server over a single TCP connection.
words.simpleDefinition: A persistent, full-duplex communication protocol allowing real-time bidirectional messaging between a client and server over a single connection. Unlike standard HTTP requests, WebSockets allow either side to push data at any time without waiting for a request.
words.longDefinition: WebSockets upgrade an HTTP connection to a long-lived socket, enabling real-time push notifications without polling. After the handshake, both client and server can send messages freely. Used for chat, live updates, and multiplayer games.
words.connector: Think of WebSocket like a phone call — once connected, both sides can speak whenever they want without re-dialing every time.
words.techContext: Contrast with HTTP polling (inefficient) and Server-Sent Events (one-way). WebSockets require different scaling strategies than stateless HTTP.
words.exampleSentence: We used WebSockets for the trading dashboard so price updates appeared instantly without the client polling every second.
words.etymology: standardized as RFC 6455 in 2011; 'socket' from BSD sockets API (1983).

words.shortDefinition: A query language for APIs that lets clients request exactly the data they need.
words.simpleDefinition: API query language and runtime allowing clients to request exactly the specific data fields they need — avoiding over-fetching and under-fetching. It provides a strongly typed schema that defines the shape and relationships of available backend data.
words.longDefinition: GraphQL provides a strongly typed schema defining available queries and mutations. Clients specify which fields to retrieve, eliminating over-fetching and under-fetching. A single endpoint handles all queries. Developed by Facebook; open-sourced in 2015.
words.connector: Think of GraphQL like a custom sandwich order — you specify exactly which ingredients you want layer by layer for one plate.
words.techContext: Contrasts with REST where each endpoint returns a fixed structure. GraphQL introduces complexity (caching, N+1 queries) but offers flexibility.
words.exampleSentence: We adopted GraphQL so the mobile app could fetch user, posts, and comments in a single request instead of hitting three REST endpoints.
words.etymology: Graph (data structure) + QL (Query Language); released by Facebook in 2015.

words.shortDefinition: Asynchronous JavaScript and XML - a technique for updating web pages without full refresh.
words.simpleDefinition: Technique enabling web pages to update content dynamically by sending and receiving data from a server in the background — without requiring a full page reload. It drastically improves user experience by enabling faster, more interactive web interfaces.
words.longDefinition: AJAX uses JavaScript to send HTTP requests in the background and update parts of the page with the response. Despite the name, JSON has largely replaced XML. AJAX enabled the shift from server-rendered pages to dynamic single-page applications.
words.connector: Think of AJAX like a waiter who refills your water glass quietly — small updates happen without you having to restart the meal.
words.techContext: Modern equivalent: fetch() API. AJAX revolutionized web UX by enabling Gmail, Google Maps, and dynamic interfaces.
words.exampleSentence: We used AJAX to load search results as the user typed, providing instant feedback without navigating to a new page.
words.etymology: coined by Jesse James Garrett in 2005; acronym for Asynchronous JavaScript and XML.

words.shortDefinition: Single Page Application - a web app that loads once and dynamically updates content via JavaScript.
words.simpleDefinition: Single Page Application — a web application that loads a single HTML page and dynamically updates content as the user interacts with it. This creates a fast, fluid experience that feels like a native mobile or desktop app.
words.longDefinition: SPAs load an initial HTML shell, then use JavaScript to render views and fetch data via APIs. Navigation happens client-side without full page reloads. Frameworks: React, Vue, Angular. Trade-offs: faster interactions, worse SEO (unless server-side rendered), larger initial bundle.
words.connector: Think of an SPA like a digital magazine — the cover loads once and you flip through pages instantly without leaving the app.
words.techContext: SPAs blur the line between web and native apps. Client-side routing, state management, and code splitting are essential patterns.
words.exampleSentence: Our SPA felt snappy because navigation was instant, but SEO suffered until we added server-side rendering.
words.etymology: term emerged around 2002-2005 as AJAX-driven apps became common; popularized by frameworks in 2010s.

words.shortDefinition: Server-Side Rendering - generating HTML on the server for each request.
words.simpleDefinition: Process where a web server generates the complete HTML for a page upon each request — rather than relying on the client's browser to build the UI. It improves SEO and performance for slow user devices.
words.longDefinition: SSR produces fully-formed HTML on the server, sending complete pages to the client. Good for SEO, fast first paint, and works without JavaScript. Contrasts with CSR (client-side rendering). Modern frameworks (Next.js, Nuxt) support both SSR and static generation.
words.connector: Think of SSR like a pre-made meal — the kitchen does all the work upfront so the food arrives ready to eat immediately.
words.techContext: SSR improves SEO and perceived performance but increases server load and complexity. Often combined with client-side hydration.
words.exampleSentence: We added SSR to our React app with Next.js so search engines could crawl product pages and improve rankings.
words.etymology: term emerged to distinguish traditional server rendering from client-side SPAs; formalized in modern frameworks 2010s.

words.shortDefinition: An HTTP response instructing the client to request a different URL.
words.simpleDefinition: Server response instructing a browser to automatically request a different URL — used for moving pages, enforcing HTTPS, or managing successful form submissions. Redirects ensure users always land on the correct, current version of a resource.
words.longDefinition: Redirects use 3xx status codes: 301 (permanent, cached by browsers), 302 (temporary, not cached), 303 (after POST → GET), 307 (temporary, preserve method). The Location header specifies the target URL. Used for URL changes, HTTPS enforcement, and post-login flows.
words.connector: Think of a redirect like a mail forwarding address — the post office sends your letters to wherever you actually live now.
words.techContext: Search engines treat 301s as canonical URL changes. 302s preserve original URLs. Choose carefully based on intent.
words.exampleSentence: We used a 301 redirect from the old domain to the new one so search rankings transferred over.
words.etymology: re- ("again") + direct from Latin directus ("straight"); first appeared in HTTP/1.0 (1996).

codeExample.code:
HTTP/1.1 301 Moved Permanently
Location: https://example.com/new-path

# Client automatically requests new URL

words.shortDefinition: The actual data transmitted in an HTTP request or response body.
words.simpleDefinition: The actual data content carried within an HTTP request or response body — excluding transport headers and metadata. It represents the primary information, like a JSON object or image file, being exchanged between client and server.
words.longDefinition: The payload is the message content, distinct from headers and metadata. In HTTP requests, it's the JSON, XML, or form data sent to the server. In responses, it's the data returned to the client. Size limits, encoding, and content type are key considerations.
words.connector: Think of a payload like the package inside a shipping box — the box is the header, but the payload is what you ordered.
words.techContext: Large payloads impact performance. Use pagination, compression (gzip), or streaming for big datasets. Content-Type header describes payload format.
words.exampleSentence: The request failed because the JSON payload exceeded the server's 10MB limit for POST bodies.
words.etymology: from pay + load; originally aeronautical (revenue-generating cargo); adopted in networking for actual data vs. overhead.

codeExample.code:
POST /api/users HTTP/1.1
Content-Type: application/json
Content-Length: 52

{"name": "Ada Lovelace", "email": "ada@example.com"}
# ↑ This JSON is the payload
